org: CREDA Colorado River Energy Distributors Association
position_summary: Focuses on hydropower-related aspects of LTEMP experiments; opposes a fall HFE that includes water bypassing generators and calls for fuller WAPA/Basin Fund analysis and TWG engagement.

org: CREDA Colorado River Energy Distributors Association
position_summary: Provides comment on Reclamation's NOI to prepare a SEIS for the 2007 Interim Guidelines, noting CREDA's members represent CRSP customers and have a unique interest in shortage sharing and coordinated operations.

org: CREDA, Colorado River Energy Distributors Association
position_summary: Requests the SEIS analyze cumulative effects on hydropower production, include hydropower alongside water elements, involve WAPA and an interdisciplinary team, assess grid reliability and impacts to power customers (including tribes), and consider legislative/regulatory strategies to rebalance power obligations.
quote: The scope of the SEIS must analyze and consider the cumulative effects on the System’s hydropower production

org: CREDA, Colorado River Energy Distributors Association
position_summary: Recommends that hydropower be added as one of the Operational Topics in the 7.D. review, urging Reclamation to consider impacts to CRSP generation, firm electric service customers, and basin fund issues in the Interim Guidelines retrospective.
quote: As federal hydropower production is an integral part of the stated purposes of the 20 07 Interim Guidelines, CREDA recommends that hydropower be added as one of the Operational Topics described in the 7.D. review.

position_summary: Urges USBR to reduce reliance on hydrology ensembles based solely on historical observations, prioritize temperature-adjusted ensembles that account for future warming, investigate GCM/downscaling artifacts, and ensure sampling of plausible dry-end scenarios to guard against underestimated drought risks.

org: Department of Energy, Western Area Power Administration, Colorado River Storage Project
position_summary: WAPA-CRSP supports including the NPS/USFWS joint alternatives in the Post-2026 EIS; asks Reclamation to consider LTEMP cumulative impacts, target Lake Powell ≥3,570 ft on Oct 1, maintain Pearce Ferry fish barrier, consider combined Mead/Powell accounting, and shift Powell accounting to April 1.
quote: Target Lake Powell elevation to be at or above 3,570 feet on October 1.

position_summary: Asks that post-2026 operational guidelines prioritize Tribal Nations' water rights and infrastructure, increase flexibility, support conservation and ecosystem preservation, engage in Upper Basin planning, consider permanent water-use cuts, improve tributary and monitoring infrastructure, and fund restoration.
quote: Overall, the post-2026 Operational plan need to prioritize Tribal Nations water rights and infrastructure, increasing flexibility, understanding variability in the face of climate change, and ensure conservation and ecosystem preservation.

position_summary: Requests the Draft 7.D Review be expanded to evaluate impacts to Lake Powell and the Upper Basin, analyze alternative Powell operations to protect Upper Basin uses and planned development, and examine potential future impacts from Lower Basin ICS withdrawals.
quote: I do not think that the 7.D Review adequately addressed impacts to Lake Powell. It could have contrasted alternate operations of Powell that would consider protection of UB uses now and planned future development.

position_summary: Opposes operations that drive Lake Powell to dangerously low levels; urges Reclamation to evaluate alternatives that force Lower Basin compliance, consider evaporation and tributary accruals, limit Powell releases to protect Lee Ferry deliveries, and use Long-Range Operating Criteria for No Action analysis.

position_summary: EPA requests Reclamation revise the Final SEIS to incorporate environmental justice (EO 14096) and Justice40 considerations, address Tribal impacts and historic inequities, improve government-to-government consultation, assess Tribal water rights, and include air-quality mitigation (Salton Sea) and post-2026 planning guidance.
quote: EPA identified areas for improvement in the analysis and recommends these be addressed in the Final SEIS.

position_summary: No policy position visible in this excerpt; only signature/affiliation is shown.
quote: ervan cy Denver Water District

quote: To ensure the availability of reliable, affordable irrigation water supplies to Western farmers and ranchers.

position_summary: Expresses concern that its allocation was conditioned on construction of a dam and relocation of part of the reservation, implying objection to such conditions.

position_summary: Requests post-2026 EIS protect environmental and cultural needs, analyze reaches below Imperial Dam, oppose out-of-priority cuts to tribal water rights, support extending ICS mechanisms and compensated forbearance to enable tribal participation.

org: Front Range Water Council (Denver Water, Northern Water, Pueblo Water, Aurora Water, Colorado Springs Utilities, the Southeastern Colorado Water Conservancy District, Twin Lakes Reservoir and Canal Company)
position_summary: Supports the proposed action's purpose and hydrology assumptions but requests additional safeguards to protect Lake Powell (including allowing releases below 6.0 MAF to maintain 3,500 ft), inclusion/reanalysis of Alternatives 1 and 2 with additional 2023–24 shortages, accounting for Lower Basin system losses, and limiting Secretary extraordinary-operations scope.

position_summary: Urges Bureau to develop Post-2026 strategies that plan for hotter, drier, and more variable hydrology, ensure flexibility to deliver predictable, reliable water supplies, and pursue collaborative community solutions.

position_summary: Urges DOI/Bureau of Reclamation to include the Salton Sea in Post-2026 operational guidelines; opposes in-basin/perimeter brine lake approach and supports seawater importation as a feasible restoration solution.

position_summary: Criticizes mismanagement of Salton Sea efforts and urges adoption of the submitter's architectural restoration plan (synchronized projects: desalination, geothermal, hydro, solar) and formation of a special committee to implement it; opposes current unsynchronized SSMP approach.
quote: Therefore, it is imperative that the architectural plan (my proposal) is accepted and then focuses on its implementation which includes many synchronized projects (hydro, solar, geothermal, desalinization, etc.).

position_summary: Opposes the ‘In‑land/Perimeter/Brine Lake’ approach; urges seawater importation and full Salton Sea restoration using the author's geothermal/solar/pipeline technologies and compliance with state restoration orders; contends their plan was suppressed.
quote: The “In-land Solution” or “Perimeter/Brine Lake” is just the opposite of tackling the Climate Crisis.

position_summary: Requests that DOI/Bureau of Reclamation evaluate and adopt its Salton Sea restoration proposal, initiate a global contest respecting patent law, ensure transparency, investigate mismanagement, and harmonize projects to reduce salinity, restore habitat, and generate revenue.
quote: I respectfully urge the DEPARTMENT OF THE INTERIOR / Bureau of Reclamation, as higher authority, to get engaged in solving the problem of the Salton Sea

position_summary: Urges the Bureau to include the Salton Sea in Post-2026 operational consideration, to speedily evaluate the submitter's proposal, and to open an investigation into alleged local agency wrongdoing.
quote: I respectfully urge members of the Department of the Interior – Bureau of Reclamation to include the Salton Sea in consideration for the Development of Post-2026 Operational Guidelines and Strategies.

position_summary: Asked California Secretary John Laird to consider terminating a Memorandum of Understanding before Sept 30, 2016 based on new information not previously presented.
quote: asking him to consider terminating Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) before September 30, 2016

position_summary: Requested that Secretary John Laird inform Governor Brown about the existence of the submitter's Salton Sea restoration proposal.
quote: asking him to inform Governor Brown about the existence of my proposal.

quote: there is a group of influential people who would like to get rid of the Salton Sea

position_summary: Submitted a five‑page letter summarizing problems with the Salton Sea and proposing a solution, urging recipients to read and consider the proposal.
quote: In that letter, I have summarized problems and a solution. Please read it.

position_summary: Strongly opposed the SSMP 'Current Course of Action' Perimeter Lake and related dust suppression projects, arguing they rest on a false assumption excluding seawater importation and risk ecological disaster.
quote: I strongly oppose the “Current Course of Action” which is the “Perimeter Lake” and related dust suppression projects

position_summary: Commented on the Notice for public comment on the Basin Plan, reminding the Board of the submitter's proposal and its relevance to preserving and enhancing regional water quality.
quote: I reminded them about the existence of my proposal and what it does

position_summary: Provided a six‑page letter to Salton Sea Task Force members summarizing problems and offering a solution for Salton Sea restoration.
quote: In that letter, I have summarized problems and a solution. Please read it.

position_summary: Told the Lithium Valley Commission that the proposal does not interfere with lithium extraction from geothermal brine and can supply needed water, uniting both projects successfully.
quote: my proposal does not interfere with the extraction of lithium from geothermal brine if there is a dike built around the desired area

position_summary: Comments on the Salton Sea Long Range Plan urging CNRA not to rely on the UCSC Panel report, calling that report fraudulent and advocating consideration of the submitter's proposal.

position_summary: As public comment on the Long-Range Plan draft, urged CNRA‑SSMP to stop the current course of action, calling it a 'drive in the wrong direction' and pointing out factual problems.

position_summary: Responded to the US Army Corps of Engineers, urging thorough study of the proposal and resisting pressure from local interests seeking to eliminate the Salton Sea.

position_summary: Requests review and acceptance of the submitter's architectural plan to restore the Salton Sea and calls for formation of a special committee to implement synchronized projects (hydro, solar, geothermal, desalination).
quote: it is imperative that the architectural plan (my proposal) is accepted and then focuses on its implementation

position_summary: Opposes the North Lake Pilot Demonstration Project and related in‑basin/Perimeter-Brine solutions; calls the $19.25M pilot misguided and a 'scam', urges halting the current course, enforcing state policy, and pursuing feasible long-term restoration (e.g., seawater importation).
quote: There is no logical reason for spending $19.25 million to supposedly understand what kind of water quality and water supply was available.

position_summary: Urges the Bureau of Reclamation to include the Salton Sea in post-2026 operations analysis, reopen investigation, and promptly evaluate Geothermal Worldwide's detailed restoration proposal to avoid environmental, public health, and economic harms from losing the Salton Sea.

position_summary: Urges adoption of an architectural plan to restore and refill the Salton Sea by importing seawater and using geothermal/solar desalination; requests inclusion of the author as consultant, a special implementation commission, and an investigation into parties opposing restoration.

position_summary: Urges Reclamation to include the Salton Sea in Post-2026 operational guidelines; opposes the current smaller-lake/perimeter-brine approach and related dust-suppression projects; supports seawater importation and calls for expert, creative planning to avert ecological and public-health disaster.
quote: I respectfully urge the Department of the Interior / Bureau of Reclamation to include the Salton Sea in consideration for the Development of Post-2026 Operational Guidelines and Strategies.

org: Getches-Wilkinson Center for Natural Resources, Energy, and the Environment, Colorado School of Law

position_summary: Requests that all 38 sovereign governments (U.S., seven basin states, and 30 basin tribes) be included as equal sovereigns whenever the U.S. meets with basin state principals.

quote: We suggest that the basin managers take the naturalist approach to solve the water scarcity issue that this nation created.

position_summary: Opposes state-dominated process; requests a robust, basin-wide EIS that protects tribal water rights and ecosystems, analyzes worst-case climate declines, considers dam decommissioning/bypass, prevents non-native fish passage, and consolidates ESA consultation across dams.

position_summary: Urges Reclamation to incorporate new science, address system vulnerabilities and climate adaptation, include equity and tribal knowledge, and analyze alternatives that reflect likely aridification — including reduced reservoir storage and significant hydropower reductions or abandoning Lake Powell storage.

position_summary: Requests the EIS fully analyze direct, indirect, and cumulative effects of Reclamation operations and evaluate a range of holistic, adaptive alternatives that prioritize long-term sustainability, mitigate climate-driven scarcity, address system vulnerabilities, and enhance stakeholder, tribal, and public engagement.

position_summary: Calls for a Post-2026 EIS term that enables investment in new technologies and augmentation while allowing flexibility to respond to climate change; emphasizes engagement with Tribes, Mexico, and stakeholders and reserves rights to provide further comments.

position_summary: Opposes depleting Lake Powell and Lake Mead for hydropower or urban/industrial uses; urges alternative power sources (e.g., fusion) and consideration of desalination; offers help to protect lakes.

org: Mohave County Board of Supervisors (Mohave County, Arizona)

position_summary: Requests climate-oriented, science-based modeling; increased management flexibility, climate adaptation and infrastructure resilience; environmental justice analysis; tribal and Mexico coordination; evaluation of robust alternatives and demand management; and that EPA serve as a cooperating agency.
quote: The EPA respectfully requests to be a cooperating agency to provide support to Reclamation regarding the identification and analysis of issues to be addressed in the Draft EIS.

position_summary: Requests early coordination in the EIS process to accommodate Section 7 and 10(a)(1)(B) permit timelines; maintain LCR MSCP partnership and conservation lands; reexamine baseline conditions and offer assistance throughout the EIS.
quote: Please include the Service as early as possible so that we can provide input and be responsive to time intensive aspects of project requirements.

position_summary: Recommends DRO operations remain within suggested Colorado pikeminnow baseflow release targets after summer to avoid substantially reducing nursery habitats and displacing juvenile pikeminnow.
quote: which is why the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service recommends Operations remain in the suggested pikeminnow baseflow release targets after summer.

position_summary: Supports a compromise Trinity River release schedule balancing environmental flows and off-basin uses; proposes specific annual release volumes for normal, dry, and critically dry years to maintain fish habitat.
quote: 340,000 acre-feet annual fishery release in normal years; 220,000 acre-feet fishery release in dry years; 140,000 acre-feet fishery release in critically dry years.

position_summary: Requests more analysis and information to ensure ESA and National Wildlife Refuge requirements are met: quantify projected minimum releases (flow rate, timing, duration, stage changes); assess downstream impacts to refuges, Salton Sea water levels, salinity, exposed lakebed, wetlands, and listed species; and revise SEIS accordingly.
quote: The Service requests information on the projected minimum releases for the alternatives, including flow rate, seasonal timing, duration, and related river stage elevation decreases.

org: U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and National Park Service
position_summary: Requests Reclamation integrate NPS and USFWS priority concepts into Post‑2026 EIS alternatives: protect native species and water quality, use combined Powell‑Mead storage, prioritize higher Powell elevations and Powell releases, maintain Lake Mead below 1135' where feasible, mimic natural hydrographs, and retain Upper Basin flexibilities.
quote: A critical aspect of our joint concepts and example operational strategies is the use of combined storage of both reservoirs in determining annual releases and potential shortages.

position_summary: Urges federal action to adopt SEIS recommendation 3 (equal-percentage cuts) to save 2–4 million acre-feet per year, advocates using water/funding for cloud seeding rather than forced fallowing, and criticizes irrigated cash crops' water use.
quote: I suggest that all the water that was once earmarked for California's farmers of the Imperial Irrigation District to fallow their land be used for cloud seeding.

org: Undersigned Tribes within the Colorado River Basin
position_summary: Requests meaningful, government-to-government tribal participation in developing post-2007 Colorado River management; acknowledgement and protection of tribal reserved water rights; refinement of CRSS for tribal uses; analysis and removal of barriers to tribal participation in programs like ICS; consultation with all 29 tribes.
quote: it is imperative that tribes be included in a meaningful way in decision-making for the Colorado River in the years to come.

org: Unit B Irrigation and Drainage District
position_summary: Demands an equitable, transparent post-2026 process including all stakeholders; insists plans follow the Law of the River and priority system; calls for ICS reform, prioritization of voluntary compensated conservation, and clarity/certainty for water users.
quote: The Bureau's post-2026 plans and supporting environmental analysis must follow the Law of the River.

org: United States Department of the Interior FISH AND WILDLIFE SERVICE
position_summary: Supports Reclamation's effort to develop post-2026 operational guidelines, offers assistance, and requests close coordination and detailed analyses to ensure Endangered Species Act compliance and assessment of tipping points.
quote: The Service supports this effort.

org: United States Department of the Interior NATIONAL PARK SERVICE Interior Regions 6, 7 & 8
position_summary: Requests close NPS involvement in the EIS; urges balancing supply and demand, proactively rebuilding reservoir storage using inflows, modeling worst-case hydrology beyond historical conditions, and analyzing environmental, cultural, recreational, water quality, and endangered species impacts.
quote: Balance Supply and Demand – Long-term average consumptive uses and losses should not exceed the average natural water supply.

org: UNITED STATES ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY REGION IX
position_summary: Supports Reclamation identifying and analyzing alternatives to replace expiring interim guidelines and align available supplies with deliveries to increase stability, protect public health and the environment, and provides NEPA Section 309 review recommendations for the Draft EIS.
quote: The EPA supports Reclamation in identifying and analyzing alternatives to achieve these goals.

position_summary: Requests planners not forget the Salton Sea and to import ocean water to it, warning that Colorado River cuts without ocean imports will destroy the Salton Sea.
quote: Colorado River water cuts without ocean water imports will destroy the Salton Sea.

org: UNM School of Law The Utton Transboundary Resources Center
position_summary: Urges Reclamation to establish operational 'guard rails', consider a wide range of hydrologic outcomes, improve transparency in evaporation and consumptive-use accounting, incentivize conservation (including Upper Basin banking), consider reservoir operations to reduce losses, and include tribal interests.
quote: we highly suggest shifting language in the Post-2026 Guidelines from using the word “drought” to using the word “aridification”

position_summary: Requests Post-2026 Guidelines that recognize Tribal sovereignty, ensure meaningful Tribal involvement, incorporate Indigenous knowledge and environmental values, build resilience to climate change, and provide transparent, inclusive governance to avoid crisis-driven management.
quote: It is imperative that Tribal Nations be involved in crafting workable solutions with the federal government and the states and it is time to correct the historical wrong of Tribal exclusion.

position_summary: Requests an inclusive, transparent post-2026 NEPA process that meaningfully involves Tribal Nations, recognizes reduced Colorado River flows, allows tribes to develop federal reserved water rights, and corrects historical tribal exclusion while crafting durable operational strategies.
quote: it is imperative that Tribal Nations be involved in crafting workable solutions with the federal government and the states and it is time to correct the historical wrong of Tribal exclusion

position_summary: Approves the 2023 Drought Response Operations Plan and (through its commissioners) forwards the final Plan to the Secretary for consideration and approval.
quote: have approved this 2023 Plan and direct the Upper Colorado River Commission to forward this final 2023 Plan to the Secretary for consideration and approval.

position_summary: The Upper Colorado River Commission concurs with Amendment 1 to Exhibit B of the 2022 Drought Response Operations Plan, provided the amendment is implemented consistent with the original Attachment B provisions; it notes uncertainty about 2023 releases and hydrology.
quote: convey to Reclamation their agreement with the proposed amendment, so long as the proposed amendment is implemented consistent with the original provisions of Attachment B of the 2022 Plan.

position_summary: Supports development of new Post-2026 operational guidelines for Lakes Powell and Mead; urges inclusion of Basin States, protection of Upper Basin rights, durable mechanisms to protect and rebuild storage, and actions to address supply-demand imbalance with responsive operations.
quote: The Upper Division States support the development of new operational guidelines and strategies for Lake Powell and Lake Mead (“Post-2026 Operations”) to replace the 2007 Guidelines.

org: Upper Colorado River Commission; Colorado Water Conservation Board
position_summary: Requests Reclamation issue the Lower Basin Consumptive Uses and Losses Report 2006-2022, analyze Secretary authorities, ensure post-2026 guidelines are interim, consult on 2000 ISGs modeling and No Action alternative, and involve Basin States and Tribes in processes.
quote: it is imperative that Reclamation issue the Lower Basin Consumptive Uses and Losses Report 2006-2022 at the earliest opportunity.

org: Upper Colorado River Commissioner Department of Natural Resources COLORADO Colorado Water Conservation Board Department of Natural Resources
position_summary: Submitted comments to the Bureau of Reclamation regarding post-2026 operational guidelines for the Colorado River; the substantive content is not included in this fragment.
quote: Rebecca Mitchell, Colorado’s Colorado River Commissioner | Lauren Ris, CWCB Acting Director

org: Upper Colorado River Division States of Colorado, New Mexico, Utah , and Wyoming ("Upper Division States"), through the Upper Colorado River Commission ("Commission")
position_summary: Supports adopting and implementing a Drought Response Operations Agreement to minimize Lake Powell declines, maintain hydropower and compact obligations, and coordinate Upper Basin drought response among the Upper Division States.
quote: The Parties intend through this Drought Response Operations Agreement to: 1. Prepare, in advance of drought conditions, drought response operations that will minimize the risk of low water storage conditions at Lake Powell

org: Upper Colorado River Division States of Colorado, New Mexico, Utah, and Wyoming, Upper Colorado River Commission, Secretary of the Interior
position_summary: Parties agree to coordinated drought-response operations using CRSPA Initial Units to prevent Lake Powell falling below 3,525 ft, prioritize Glen Canyon adjustments first, consider all initial units for releases, and ensure recovery of storage while respecting compacts, water rights, and hydropower needs.
quote: The Parties agree to implement their best efforts to coordinate and collaborate on an ongoing basis to achieve the purposes and implement the provisions of this Drought Response Operations Agreement.

org: Upper Colorado River Endangered Fish Recovery Program
position_summary: Requested experimental operations at Flaming Gorge: a spring peak flow, summer base flows to benefit Colorado pikeminnow, and a smallmouth bass flow spike; DRO operations seek to address these priorities.
quote: will address all three experimental priorities from the Recovery Program’s 2023 flow request letter

org: Upper Division States of Colorado, New Mexico, Utah, and Wyoming, Upper Colorado River Commission (UCRC)
position_summary: Acknowledges completion of 2023 DROA releases, expresses appreciation for Reclamation's collaboration, and states readiness to continue working with Reclamation and to proceed with any 2024 DROA plan in accordance with DROA and the UCRC's September 21, 2023 resolution.
quote: The Upper Division States and the UCRC stand ready to continue working with Reclamation consistent with DROA.

position_summary: Requests that the Ute Indian Tribe be seated on the Upper Colorado River Commission and that the Bureau of Indian Affairs have visible active representation in Reclamation's leadership.
quote: We strongly request that (1) the Ute Indian Tribe be seated as a participant on the Upper Colorado River Commission

position_summary: Expresses concern that Reclamation has failed to account for and protect the Tribe’s quantified reserved water rights; demands accounting, negotiations, and that Reclamation lead discussions to account for Tribal water separate from System water.
quote: Reclamation needs to initiate discussions and negotiations (i.e., take the lead) on how our Tribal water rights will be accounted for (as separate from the "System" water under negotiations), can be developed, and used.

org: Ute Indian Tribe of the Uintah and Ouray Reservation
position_summary: Requests Reclamation expand the SEIS scope to include Upper Basin tribes, analyze effects on the Ute Tribe’s Indian reserved water rights and Indian Trust Assets, acknowledge federal trust obligations, and ensure protection and equitable consideration of tribal water development and legal rights.
quote: Our Tribe is completely excluded from any evaluation, analysis, or consideration on the critical issues before Colorado River water users today.
doc_url: https://www.usbr.gov/ColoradoRiverBasin/documents/NearTermColoradoRiverOperations/PublicComments/Tribes/115_Ute Indian Tribe of the Uintah and Ouray Reservation_508.pdf

position_summary: Requests compensation for Tribes to forgo or bypass their water, calls for tracking Tribal water contributions, incorporation of hydrology into Lower Basin modeling, accounting for evaporation/transit losses, and protection of water needs for energy projects.
quote: Tribes must be compensated for unused water and compensated to refrain from developing their water.

position_summary: Supports tribal inclusion, requests upholding federal trust responsibility to preserve reserved water rights, incorporation of indigenous knowledge, opposes using Upper Basin reservoirs in Basin management and reductions in Upper Basin consumptive use, and seeks mechanisms for tribal consumption accounting.
quote: the UMUT needs the federal government to uphold its trust responsibility in preserving and protecting tribes' federal reserved water rights and use in perpetuity.

position_summary: States WAPA can meet BA, reliability, reserve pool, and black-start obligations under modeled hydrological conditions and does not anticipate significant change to power deliveries from the proposed DROA in WY 2023/24; notes customers are taking voluntary energy reductions.
quote: WAPA does not anticipate significant change as a result of the proposed DROA plan in WY 2023/24.

position_summary: Supports drought response operations when needed to protect Lake Powell target elevation; requests specific scheduling and mitigation measures to minimize impacts to hydropower, the Basin Fund, and the bulk electric system.
quote: WAPA supports Drought Response Operations when they are necessary to protect the Target Elevation at Lake Powell.

position_summary: States Colorado River operations directly affect Washington County; supports Colorado River Authority of Utah's basin-wide consensus alternative and incorporates the Authority's comments; outlines local conservation and water-supply planning.
quote: The District hereby endorses and incorporates the Authority's comments on the NOi.

position_summary: Submits formal comments on the Revised Draft SEIS, endorses and incorporates the Upper Colorado River Commission's comments, and requests Reclamation consider impacts to Washington County's Colorado River-connected water supplies and ongoing conservation and supply plans.
quote: The district hereby endorses and incorporates the Upper Colorado River Commission's comments on the Revised Draft SEIS.

org: Waste To Power And Water llc, WTPAW
position_summary: Proposes the SEA-2-SEA aqueduct and hybrid geothermal-desalination project to deliver millions of acre-feet of freshwater from the Sea of Cortez to the Salton Sea; requests permits, federal funding, agency agreements, tribal and community collaboration, and support.
quote: SEA2SEA project aims delivery over seven million acre-feet of freshwater per year.

position_summary: Submitting comments in response to the Bureau of Reclamation's Notice of Intent to prepare an Environmental Impact Statement on development of Post-2026 operational guidelines for the Colorado River reservoirs.
quote: Re: Notice of Intent (NOi) to Prepare an Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) on the Development of Post-2026 Operational Guidel

position_summary: Asks that the largest users, especially Southwest agriculture, pay for building pipelines from Midwest sources to the Colorado River or face prohibition; suggests moving water and reducing evaporation by altering flows between Lake Powell and Lake Mead.
quote: But it would be fair to make the biggest users, especially southwest agriculture, pay at least their fair share of the water needs to build pipelines from midwest sources to the Colorado river

org: Wellton-Mohawk Irrigation and Drainage District, Yuma Mesa Irrigation and Drainage District, Yuma Irrigation District, North Gila Valley Irrigation and Drainage District (collectively, the Districts)
position_summary: Supports voluntary, compensated conservation (most aspects of the Proposed Alternative) and flexibility for local districts/growers; opposes expedited actions that could override legal priorities or NEPA; demands the Bureau abide by the Law of the River, APA, NEPA, and equitably apportion middle-priority entitlements.
quote: the Bureau must at all times allocate water deliveries and reductions according to the priority system encoded in the Law of the River

position_summary: Requests Lake Powell and Lake Mead be placed on the same operational period (preferably the water year) because current differing calendar/water year guidelines harm Lake Mead and create misaligned releases.
quote: Why can we not be on the same operational period? in my opinion, would be the water year?

position_summary: WAPA requests revisions: improve cumulative-impact analysis for hydropower and bypass risk relative to Minimum Power Pool (MPP), correct inaccurate ancillary-services/revenue language, clarify figures/tables, and quantify yearly instances and volumes of bypass.
quote: Cumulative impacts are insufficiently identified and considered for the hydropower resource.

position_summary: Proposes replacing WAPA-199 with WAPA-206 (similar structure); expects low firming expenses and anticipates limited impacts to the CRSP Basin Fund under the DRO scenarios described.
quote: Western Area Power Administration (WAPA) proposes to replace this rate with WAPA-206, which, as proposed, will have a similar structure to WAPA-199.

org: Western Area Power Administration Colorado River Storage Project (CRSP) Management Center
position_summary: Requests that the Draft 7.D. report include Glen Canyon and federal hydropower production data (annual/monthly generation, capacity, economic value), offers to provide data, and asks for a subsection/table comparing hydropower to reservoir operations, arguing the report is incomplete without it.
quote: WAPA believes any report that strives to consider the effect of water storage on Lake Powell and Lake Mead that does not include a robust discussion of the effects on hydropower production is necessarily deficient.

position_summary: Supports WRA's five principles: reduce Basin water use by 25%, plan for less water, protect flows and ecosystems, include Tribes equitably, and ensure stakeholder participation.
quote: Reduce water use across the Basin by 25%.

position_summary: Urges robust Tribal participation; supports a holistic, basin‑wide approach to post-2026 guidelines that protects environmental flows, considers storage/accounts beyond Powell and Mead, and coordinates with parallel processes (demand management, Mexico, Salton Sea).
quote: WRA is hopeful that a process will be determined for robust Tribal participation.

position_summary: Supports a broad, inclusive Post-2026 EIS; urges holistic, proactive, adaptive guidelines that reduce Colorado River consumption (at least 25%), include Tribes, protect environmental flows, expand scope beyond Lake Powell and Lake Mead, and use best available science.
quote: Cities, farms, ranches, and businesses must take steps to cut water consumption by at least 25%

org: Wild Arizona: Grand Canyon Wildlands Council
position_summary: Requests EIS alternatives that prioritize ecosystem integrity, tribal engagement, adaptive management, HFEs, maintenance of in-stream flows, incentives for conservation, and inclusion of NPS and AMWG as cooperating/engaged parties.
quote: Manage with the water we get, not the water we wish to get.

position_summary: Advocates for attention to the Salton Sea and a SEA-2-SEA WTPAW project involving the Salton Sea and seven states; suggests regional cooperation on the issue.
quote: the Salton Sea Lake and 7 States.

position_summary: Advocates for responsible management of the Colorado River and its resources in post-2026 operations, representing Wyoming's interests.
quote: managing this critical resource.

position_summary: Wyoming asks Reclamation to ensure the final report recognizes and protects Upper Basin interests—maintaining coordinated operations that avoid Upper Basin curtailment, limits additional Lower Basin flexibilities that could increase risk, and better documents hydropower impacts; emphasizes negotiation and cooperation.
quote: The draft report primarily focuses on the Lower Basin, not on the protection afforded to the Upper Basin by storage in Lake Powell.

position_summary: Requests post-2026 operational guidelines that plan for future hydrology and growth, allocate climate risk fairly between basins, protect Upper Basin conserved water in Lake Powell, prefer small incremental releases with conservation, and institute fair accounting for system losses and structural deficit.
quote: Use small, incremental releases balanced with conservation to meet shortages, rather than large-volume, tier-based releases.

position_summary: Requests an additional 500 acre-feet per year to support a permanent tribal homeland.
quote: The Tribe has requested an additional allocation of 500 acre-feet per year, for maintenance of a permanent tribal homeland.

position_summary: Urges the Secretary ensure tribes with CAP allocations can equitably participate in operational strategies and that practical barriers to participation are addressed.
quote: The Secretary must ensure that the Nation and other tribes with CAP allocations are able to equitably participate

position_summary: Urges Reclamation to honor the United States' trust responsibility by protecting Tribal Colorado River deliveries (including CAP entitlements), providing direct assistance to replace lost water supplies, and engaging in consultation to safeguard Tribal health and economies.
quote: assistance should be directly provided to Tribes to replace those water supplies through other means

position_summary: Provides scoping comments, reserves legal rights, and requests continued engagement with Reclamation to protect the Colorado River and collaborate with stakeholders on post-2026 operations.
quote: The Association appreciates the opportunity to provide these scoping comments and looks forward to working cooperatively with Reclamation and other stakeholders to continue to protect the Colorado River.

position_summary: Provides comments on the scope of the forthcoming EIS for post-2026 operational guidelines for Lake Powell and Lake Mead, requesting consideration of specific operational guidelines, strategies, and related issues affecting Yuma County water users.
